original_abstract:
Modern state-of-the-art object detectors frequently utilize detection proposals to constrain the search space for objects, moving away from computationally expensive exhaustive sliding window paradigms. While these proposal methods are widely adopted, the specific trade-offs they introduce regarding detection accuracy and efficiency have remained poorly understood. In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of twelve prominent proposal methods and four baselines, analyzing their performance across several critical dimensions: proposal repeatability, ground truth recall on the PASCAL, ImageNet, and MS COCO datasets, and their downstream impact on DPM, R-CNN, and Fast R-CNN detectors.

Our rigorous analysis reveals that maximizing ground truth recall at low overlap thresholds is insufficient for high-quality object detection; instead, improving proposal localization accuracy is equally vital. Based on these findings, we propose a new evaluation metric, Average Recall (AR), which accounts for both recall and localization quality. We demonstrate that AR exhibits a remarkably high correlation with final detection performance, offering a more reliable proxy than traditional metrics. Finally, we highlight the common strengths and weaknesses of current approaches and provide actionable insights for selecting and optimizing proposal methods for different detection frameworks.
humanized_abstract:
Recently popular state-of-the-art object detection algorithms utilize detection proposals to improve computational efficiency, while avoiding full search or brute force sliding window approaches. Even though proposal region proposals have been widely used, the resulting effects such as object detection accuracy and computational complexity remain poorly explored. Herein, the authors investigate 12 regions-proposal methods and 4 baselines in great detail regarding how they compare with respect to region repetition scores, ground truth region recall on three benchmark datasets (PASCAL, ImageNet, MS COCO) and the effects on their associated object detection algorithms: the Detection Patch Model (DPM) as well as the Region-based Convolutional Neural Network (R-CNN) and its Fast variant (Fast R-CNN).

Although high recall for ground truth instances at a low overlap threshold may not lead to accurate object detection, our experiments show that it is more important to focus on improving the precision of proposal localization. As a result, we introduce a new evaluation metric referred to as Average Recall (AR). We show that the AR scores have strong correlation with the final object detection performance, much stronger than the commonly used metrics. We reveal the strengths and weaknesses among various object proposal methods and make recommendations for designing and optimizing object proposal methods given a specific object detection approach.

document.input:
Modern state-of-the-art object detectors frequently utilize detection proposals to constrain the search space for objects, moving away from computationally expensive exhaustive sliding window paradigms. While these proposal methods are widely adopted, the specific trade-offs they introduce regarding detection accuracy and efficiency have remained poorly understood. In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of twelve prominent proposal methods and four baselines, analyzing their performance across several critical dimensions: proposal repeatability, ground truth recall on the PASCAL, ImageNet, and MS COCO datasets, and their downstream impact on DPM, R-CNN, and Fast R-CNN detectors.

Our rigorous analysis reveals that maximizing ground truth recall at low overlap thresholds is insufficient for high-quality object detection; instead, improving proposal localization accuracy is equally vital. Based on these findings, we propose a new evaluation metric, Average Recall (AR), which accounts for both recall and localization quality. We demonstrate that AR exhibits a remarkably high correlation with final detection performance, offering a more reliable proxy than traditional metrics. Finally, we highlight the common strengths and weaknesses of current approaches and provide actionable insights for selecting and optimizing proposal methods for different detection frameworks.
